title: SEGMENT 13 — The cancer revolution — checkpoint inhibitors

lines.text: SEGMENT 13 — The cancer revolution — checkpoint inhibitors

I want to take this segment slowly, because it covers the discovery that arguably represents the single most important change in cancer treatment in fifty years, and it won the Nobel Prize in 2018 for James Allison and Tasuku Honjo.
We're talking about checkpoint inhibitors.
To understand what a checkpoint inhibitor does, you need to understand a basic feature of the immune system.
Your T cells — which are the immune cells responsible for recognizing and killing infected or abnormal cells — have, built into them, a set of safety brakes.
Receptors on the T cell surface that, when engaged, tell the cell to stop.
To not attack.
To stand down.
Why would evolution build brakes into your killer cells?
Because uncontrolled T cell activity is dangerous.
Without brakes, T cells would attack your own tissues — autoimmunity — and would over-respond to mild threats, causing collateral damage.
The brakes prevent friendly fire.
Two of the most important brake systems involve receptors called CTLA-4 and PD-1.
When these receptors are engaged by their corresponding signals from other cells, the T cell is inhibited — it pulls back from attacking.
Now, here is the discovery, and it's the kind of thing that makes you appreciate evolution's cruelty and ingenuity at the same time.
Cancers, it turns out, learned to exploit these brakes.
Tumor cells often display, on their surface, molecules that engage PD-1 on T cells — the molecule is called PD-L1.
When a T cell approaches a tumor and recognizes its abnormal proteins, the T cell is initially activated and ready to attack.
But then PD-L1 on the tumor surface engages PD-1 on the T cell, slamming on the brake, telling the T cell to stand down.
The T cell, obedient to its safety mechanism, pulls back.
The tumor has, in effect, displayed a counterfeit "I'm safe, don't attack" badge, and the T cell — which can't tell the difference — believes it.
The cancer hides behind the very safety mechanism the immune system uses to prevent autoimmunity.
Here's the analogy.
Imagine you've trained an attack dog to recognize burglars, but the dog also has a strict command — when someone shows it a particular badge, it has to stand down.
Now imagine the burglars have figured this out, and they all wear fake badges.
The dog approaches, recognizes the burglar, gets ready to lunge — and then sees the badge, and obediently sits.
The burglar walks past.
The training is working perfectly; it's just being exploited.
The therapeutic question becomes: how do you get the dog to ignore the badge?
Answer: you cover the badge with tape, so the dog can't see it.
The training is still intact, the safety mechanism is still functional in principle, but you've temporarily disabled the brake against this specific encounter.
A checkpoint inhibitor is an antibody that does exactly that.
It binds either to PD-1 on the T cell, or to PD-L1 on the tumor, physically covering the receptor or its ligand so they can't engage each other.
The brake never gets pulled.
The T cell, no longer receiving the "stand down" signal, proceeds to do what it was already trained to do — attack the cancer cell.
Notice the conceptual elegance.
The antibody is not killing the cancer.
The antibody is not delivering a drug.
The antibody is simply removing the brake the cancer has installed on the immune T cell.
The killing is done by the patient's own T cells, which were ready and willing all along — they were just being held back.
This was a radical reframe of cancer therapy.
For a century, oncology had been built on the premise of finding ways to kill cancer cells — surgery, radiation, chemotherapy, targeted small molecules.
Checkpoint inhibitors don't try to kill cancer cells.
They try to take the leash off the immune system.
And it works — in some patients, spectacularly.

Patients with metastatic melanoma, which used to be a near-uniformly fatal diagnosis, can sometimes achieve complete and durable remission with checkpoint inhibitors — meaning the cancer disappears and stays gone for years.
Same for some lung cancers, some kidney cancers, some bladder cancers.
It doesn't work for every patient — only a fraction respond — but for those who respond, the responses can be far more lasting than what traditional therapies achieve.
Drugs in this class include ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4), pembrolizumab and nivolumab (anti-PD-1), and atezolizumab (anti-PD-L1).

The cost has been enormous, the side effects can be severe (because you've also released the brakes on autoimmunity — patients can develop new autoimmune conditions as a price for the cancer response), but the field has been permanently changed.
Cancer immunotherapy is now a foundational pillar of oncology, and it started with the realization that some cancers were exploiting an immune brake we'd never thought to manipulate.
